narrative: I'm sorry to deliver this news, but the law school's admission model predicted a 53% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which was enough to lead to the rejection of your application. This prediction is based on your overall profile, including your Year 1 school decile rank of 2—well below the typical student's rank around the middle of their class—your similarly low Year 3 decile rank of 2 compared to the average near 5.5, your LSAT score of 33 against the usual 36.7, your undergraduate GPA of 2.2 versus the average 3.2, low family income bracket, your background as a Black male pursuing full-time studies. These details paint a picture of someone who has faced challenges in academic performance and standardized testing relative to most applicants. The model's decision was driven most strongly by your Year 3 decile rank, which had the biggest influence in pushing the prediction toward expecting bar exam failure. Next in importance came your LSAT score, which also nudged the outlook toward higher risk of not passing. Your Year 1 decile rank followed closely, reinforcing that early academic struggles mattered a lot here. Factors like your family income, racial background, undergraduate GPA, full-time status, and gender had progressively smaller influences, all leaning in the same direction of increased failure risk compared to typical students. For context, most students in the dataset are White, come from higher income brackets, score better on the LSAT and GPA, and rank higher in their school deciles, which helps them get favorable predictions. While demographics like race and gender played a role, they weren't the top drivers—your academic metrics carried the most weight. To improve your chances next time, focus on what you can control: retake the LSAT to boost that score closer to or above the average, as it's a key changeable factor that strongly sways these predictions. Building a stronger recent academic record through additional coursework or certifications could help offset lower past deciles and GPA. With dedicated bar exam prep tailored to your learning style, you can absolutely shift the odds in your favor—many students overcome similar profiles through targeted effort. Keep pushing forward; your determination will make the difference.
